evidence: zip-create return type is `Promise<Blob[]>` (see `async run(...): Promise<Blob[]>` in the code) — it returns an array of Blobs. But output.mime is 'application/zip' and description says 'Compress one or more files into a ZIP archive.' — a single ZIP archive, not multiple. The code returns a single-element array `[new File([outputBlob], filename, { type: 'application/zip' })]`, so functionally it works, but the output contract signals `multiple: false` by omission.
finding: Return type is Blob[] (array) which is inconsistent with the single-archive promise. If the runner expects Blob[], this is harmless; if it expects a single Blob, this could break. The inconsistency should be resolved.
suggestion: Change return type to Promise<Blob> to match the single-archive output contract, or explicitly set output.multiple = false to clarify intent.

evidence: zip-info outputMime is 'application/json' and the result object contains nested objects/arrays: `files` is an array of `ZipEntryInfo` objects, and the top-level object has fields like `entries`, `totalUncompressed`, `totalCompressed`, `compressionRatio` alongside the nested `files` array. The runner only renders flat one-level objects readably; nested arrays/objects will show raw JSON code.

suggestion: Flatten the most useful info into a one-level object (e.g. entries, totalUncompressed, totalCompressed, compressionRatio) and either move per-file data to a separate output or accept raw JSON display for the nested part.

evidence: zip-remove default pattern is '**/.env' and default removeDirectories is true. If a user drags in a ZIP expecting to simply remove a few entries but does not configure the parameters, the tool silently removes all .env files and their parent directories — a destructive default that surprises users who just wanted to inspect the tool.
finding: Default pattern removes a security-sensitive file type automatically. A user who doesn't configure params may get a cleansed archive they didn't intend to modify.
suggestion: Make pattern required (no default and validate it is set explicitly) or change the default to something harmless like '*/.DS_Store'.

evidence: zip-remove and zip-flatten re-compress with DEFLATE level 6 even if the original entries were stored uncompressed or used a different compression. A ZIP of already-compressed files (e.g. JPEGs) can grow because DEFLATE adds overhead without size benefit. The code does not check whether the output is smaller — it always re-compresses.
finding: Both tools can silently return a larger ZIP than the input by always re-compressing (DEFLATE level 6) regardless of entry type or original compression method.
suggestion: Detect when no entries are removed/modified (output equals input except for re-compression) and either return the original bytes or offer a STORE option for already-compressed content.

evidence: Same re-compression logic at DEFLATE level 6 in zip-flatten's `dst.generateAsync({ type: 'arraybuffer', compression: 'DEFLATE', compressionOptions: { level: 6 } }, ...)`. Files that are already compressed (JPEG, PNG, video) will typically grow under DEFLATE.
finding: zip-flatten can silently produce a larger archive by always re-compressing with DEFLATE level 6.
suggestion: Same as zip-remove: detect no-op (all entries already at root) and return original bytes, or allow STORE compression.

evidence: zip-info description says 'list entries, sizes, compression ratio, and timestamps' but reading `compressedSize` requires accessing the private `_data` property. The comment in the code says 'JSZip doesn't expose raw compressed sizes directly, so we use the internal _data field.' This is fragile and may break with JSZip version bumps. If `_data` is absent or restructured, compressedSize and compressionRatio will silently fall back to `uncompressedSize`, producing a 0% ratio and misleading users.
finding: Compressed size reporting depends on a private JSZip API that may break on dependency upgrades, silently returning wrong data.
suggestion: Add a try/catch around internal data access, or compute compressed size reliably via the library's public API (e.g. check JSZip's available public methods for size reporting).

evidence: zip-create has `params: none` in the metadata block (no params listed), but the code defines `ZipCreateParams` interface with `compression`, `compressionLevel`, and `filename` — and even has defaults and uses them in `run()`. The paramSchema is completely absent from the module object.

evidence: resize/types.ts: `mode: 'exact' | 'fit' | 'percent'; width?: number; height?: number; percent?: number;`. When mode is 'fit' or 'exact', width/height are required but typed as optional — missing them produces a runtime error instead of a clean validation. The metadata `defaults` block sets `width: 1920, height: 1080` for an input with no awareness of the input image size, making large images shrink to 1920x1080 trunk by default, which destroys the aspect ratio on 'exact' mode and silently scales many images down in 'fit' mode.
finding: The defaults `width: 1920, height: 1080` are applied to all modes including 'fit' — this means a user who does not set width/height will get their image scaled to fit within 1920x1080 even if the image was already smaller, which may surprise. More critically, the width/height/percent params are only validated at runtime with a throw, but typed as optional leading to confusing UX.
suggestion: Either make width/height required when mode is not 'percent', or change defaults to null with explicit checks that skip resizing when no dimensions are given.

finding: The tool is a transform/optimize tool (produces an image/png from image inputs) — its promise is a 'diff image' which could be bigger than the originals (e.g., diff of two identical images produces a black image larger than a JPEG). evidence: pdf-extract-images/index.ts description: 'PDF Extract Images — pulls embedded raster images out of a PDF... Coverage varies by PDF: pdfjs preserves some image objects after rendering and not others.' The code at line 63 imports from 'pdfjs-dist/legacy/build/pdf.mjs' and line 68 does GlobalWorkerOptions.workerSrc setting. The description says 'runs in your browser', cost is 'free'. However the workerSrc resolution block uses a try/catch with require.resolve — this only works in Node. In a browser environment, if window !== 'undefined', GlobalWorkerOptions.workerSrc is never set (line 67 condition is false, so the if-block is skipped entirely), meaning the pdfjs worker will be loaded from its default location (cdnjs or bundled) which is a cross-origin fetch. That fetch transmits user data (the PDF's bytes are passed to worker threads via transferable message, but the worker script location is fetched from external origin). The description says 'runs in your browser' but the worker script fetch is a network request — this is not a user-data transmission violation (C2) since it's static assets, but the description of coverage is honest about limitations. No finding.

If currency is empty string, moneyPattern returns /().../g which matches empty string at every position — allMatches becomes infinite. This is a likely user error that crashes.

suggestion: Reject empty currency parameter or default to '$' when empty.
